flashcards.back: A subset of AI that enables computers to learn from data and improve performance on tasks without being explicitly programmed.
ML paradigm where models learn from labeled data (input-output pairs) to predict outputs for new, unseen inputs.
ML paradigm where models discover patterns in unlabeled data without predefined labels or guidance.

flashcards.front: Name the two main types of Supervised Learning
flashcards.back: Classification (predicting discrete categories/classes) and Regression (predicting continuous numerical values).

flashcards.front: Name the two main types of Unsupervised Learning
flashcards.back: Clustering (grouping similar data points) and Dimensionality Reduction (reducing features while preserving structure).
Data that has both input features and corresponding correct output labels/targets used for training.
Data containing only input features without any output labels; used in unsupervised learning.
The portion of data used to train/fit the ML model; typically 70-80% of the dataset.
The portion of data used to evaluate model performance on unseen data; typically 20-30% of dataset.
Email spam detection: classifying emails as 'spam' or 'not spam' (discrete categories).
House price prediction: predicting continuous prices based on features like area, location, rooms.
Customer segmentation: grouping customers by purchasing behavior without predefined categories.
When a model learns training data too well including noise, performing poorly on new/unseen data.
When a model is too simple to capture patterns in data, performing poorly on both train and test data.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Parametric and Non-Parametric models?
flashcards.back: Parametric assume fixed form (e.g., Linear Regression); Non-Parametric adapt to data structure (e.g., Decision Trees).
